input.background: A manufacturer has observed that over the course of 10 months, the numbers of products demanded were $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_{10}$, respectively. If we assume that these are i.i.d. observations of demand.
input.question: What bound can be put on the probability that their average $\bar{T}=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{10} T_i}{10}$ is more than $3 \sigma$ products from the average demand $\mu$?

output.method: 事件发生概率及独立性
output.answer:
We obtain

$$
E(\bar{T})=\mu, \quad \operatorname{Var}(\bar{T})=\frac{\sigma^2}{10}
$$

From the Chebyshev's inequality,

$$
P\{|\bar{T}-\mu| \geq 3 \sigma\}=P\left\{\left|\frac{\bar{T}-\mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{10}}\right| \geq 3 \sqrt{10}\right\} \leq \frac{1}{(3 \sqrt{10})^2}=\frac{1}{90}
$$

meta_info.chapter: Chapter 1: Basic Probability, Statistics, and Reliability
